February 2009

I finally realized why politics and religion yield such uniquely useless discussions.

As a rule, any mention of religion on an online forum degenerates into a religious argument. Why? Why does this happen with religion and not with Javascript or baking or other topics people talk about online?

What's different about religion is that people don't feel they need to have any particular expertise to have opinions about it. All they need is strongly held beliefs, and anyone can have those. No one would want to edit the Wikipedia article on the Apache web server, but everyone wants to edit the one on God.

I think what religion and politics have in common is that they become part of people's identity, and people can never have a really clear view of something that has become part of their identity. By definition they can't afford to, because it would be too frightening.

When people say a discussion has degenerated into a flamewar, what they mean is that it has stopped being about ideas and started being about the people holding them. It's a very real change, because once it starts being about people, you cannot falsify anything. Every argument is then ultimately about whether the other person is a good or bad person, and such arguments are necessarily circular.

The most intriguing thing about this theory, if it's right, is that it explains not just the most obvious cases but also many subtler ones. Why, for example, are some people so | defensive about veganism? Veganism commonly becomes part of people's identities, so it triggers the same reactions.

I think what makes high-quality intellectual discussion possible is that the participants don't have a lot of their identity invested in the outcome. Changing your mind doesn't make you a bad person. And to the extent that that's true, people are much freer to follow the evidence wherever it leads.

The most important thing is to keep your identity small. The more labels you have for yourself, the dumber they make you.



Thanks to Sam Altman, Trevor Blackwell, Paul Buchheit, Jessica Livingston, and Robert Morris for reading drafts of this.
